1: The four eastern kings — Amraphel of Shinar (the identification with Hammurabi of Babylon is historically debated and cannot be confirmed), Arioch of Ellasar (location uncertain — possibly Larsa in Babylonia), Kedorlaomer of Elam (the Elamite kingdom east of Mesopotamia was a real major ANE power; Elamite kings periodically exercised control over Mesopotamian city-states), and Tidal of Goyim (literally "king of nations/peoples" — possibly a mixed-nation coalition leader) — represent a coalition of eastern imperial powers projecting force into the Syro-Canaanite corridor. The geography of the campaign (from Babylonia and Elam westward into Transjordan and the Jordan valley) reflects the real reach of Mesopotamian imperial power in the Middle Bronze Age.
2: The five city-state kings of the "cities of the plain" — Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboyim, Bela/Zoar — represent the local Canaanite political units of the southern Jordan valley. The five-city confederation (ḥamēš malkîm against the four eastern kings — "five against four") is a standard ANE political arrangement for small city-states facing a common threat. These five cities have already been identified in 10:19 as within the borders of Canaan — their political subjugation to an Elamite coalition is the ANE background against which Abram's rescue operation will be evaluated.
3: "All these latter kings joined forces in the Valley of Siddim (that is, the Dead Sea Valley)" — the Valley of Siddim is identified with the Dead Sea (yam hammelaḥ, Salt Sea) region. An ancient tradition holds that the cities of the plain were located at the southern end of the Dead Sea or in the now-submerged southern basin — supported by some geological and archaeological evidence showing large Bronze Age settlements on the plain's margins. The current Dead Sea landscape (hypersaline, lifeless, below sea level) contrasts starkly with the "well-watered" description of Genesis 13:10, consistent with the catastrophic transformation described in Genesis 19.
4: "For twelve years they had been subject to Kedorlaomer, but in the thirteenth year they rebelled" — the twelve-year submission / thirteenth-year rebellion pattern is the standard ANE suzerain-vassal relationship cycle: the vassal city-states pay annual tribute (ʿeḇôdāh) to the great king and eventually rebel when the imperial power weakens or the tribute burden becomes intolerable. The Amarna Letters (14th century BCE Egyptian diplomatic correspondence) document numerous instances of Canaanite city-states oscillating between submission and rebellion against their imperial overlords. The political context of Genesis 14 is authentic to the ANE power dynamics of the period.
5: Kedorlaomer's punitive campaign sweeps through the Transjordan, defeating multiple pre-Israelite peoples: the Rephaites (Rĕpāʾîm) of Ashteroth-Karnaim (giant peoples associated with Bashan — later connected to Og of Bashan, Deuteronomy 3:11; Joshua 12:4), the Zuzites of Ham (possibly the Zamzummim of Deuteronomy 2:20), the Emites of Shaveh-Kiriathaim (giant peoples of Moab, Deuteronomy 2:10-11). The campaign's geographic arc through the Transjordan encounters peoples who will later be displaced by Israel — the pre-conquest catalog maps future Israelite territory through its pre-Israelite occupants.
6: The Horites (Ḥōrî, possibly the Hurrians or cave-dwellers) in the hill country of Seir — the Edomite territory south of the Dead Sea. Deuteronomy 2:12,22 records that the Edomites (descendants of Esau) dispossessed the Horites from Seir. The campaign reaches as far south as El Paran "near the desert" — the northern Sinai boundary. The scope of the eastern coalition's campaign is extraordinary: from the Transjordan north (Ashteroth-Karnaim) to the southern desert (El Paran) — a sweep of the entire eastern and southern boundary of Canaan before attacking the Jordan valley cities.
7: En Mishpat (= Kadesh, the spring of judgment — the same Kadesh where Israel will later camp during the wilderness wandering, Numbers 13:26; Genesis 20:1) and Hazezon Tamar (= En Gedi on the western Dead Sea shore, 2 Chronicles 20:2). The Amalekites (ʿAmālēq) — a nomadic people of the Negev and Sinai who will be Israel's persistent enemy (Exodus 17:8-16; 1 Sam 15); and the Amorites of Hazezon Tamar. The geographic catalog of Genesis 14's campaign is remarkably specific — named places that appear throughout the subsequent Israelite narrative, lending the chapter the character of an authentic historical record from a tradition independent of the main Genesis source.
8: The five Canaanite city-state kings march out from their cities to the Valley of Siddim to give battle — a defensive mobilization against the returning punitive expedition. The "drawing up of battle lines" (wayyaʿarĕḵû ʾittām milḥāmāh) is the standard military engagement formula. The valley location for the battle reflects ANE military preference for open terrain where chariot warfare and massed infantry could be deployed. The five kings defending their territory against the four kings represents the local Canaanite confederation resisting Mesopotamian imperial enforcement of tribute — a political conflict into which Abram will be drawn through his kinship obligation to Lot.
9: The formal statement "four kings against five" (ʾarbaʿāh mĕlāḵîm ʾet-haḥāmiššāh) summarizes the battle in terms of the coalition sizes before describing the outcome. The literary technique of stating the numbers before the action is a characteristic biblical battle-narrative device (cf. Judges 7:3-8 Gideon's reduction from 32,000 to 300; 1 Samuel 13:5 the Philistine force enumeration). The four eastern kings (presumably better-armed and better-organized as an imperial force) will defeat the five local kings despite the numerical disadvantage — the imperial military superiority overcoming local resistance.
10: "The Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits (bôrôt bôrôt ḥēmār)" — bitumen/asphalt pits in the Dead Sea region are well attested: Josephus describes the asphalt that floated on the Dead Sea in his time (Jewish War IV.8.4); bitumen from the Dead Sea was used in ANE construction (cf. Genesis 11:3). The tar pits contributed to the defeat of Sodom and Gomorrah's kings: fleeing forces fell into the pits. The Hebrew "some of the men fell into them" (wayyippĕlû-šāmmāh, they fell there) while "the rest fled to the hills" — the military rout is not total annihilation but a chaotic dispersal, with the city-state armies broken and the kings' coalition defeated.
11: "The four kings seized all the goods (kol-rĕḵuš) of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their food" — the plunder of a defeated city-state was the standard ANE reward for military victory. The victor's appropriation of "goods and food" (the two primary wealth categories) represents both the economic penalty of defeat and the material incentive for the campaign. The eastern coalition's goal was tribute enforcement; when the vassal cities rebelled, the imperial response was direct extraction — taking what the tribute should have provided. The goods of Sodom that Abram will later refuse (vv22-23) are precisely the goods taken in this plunder.
12: "They also carried off Abram's nephew Lot and his possessions, since he was living in Sodom" — the narrative focus narrows from the general military campaign to the specific event that triggers Abram's action. Lot's capture is the consequence of his earlier choice (Genesis 13:12) to settle near and then in Sodom. He is carried off not because he is a target but because he is caught in the general plunder of Sodom. The phrase "since he was living in Sodom" (wĕhûʾ yōšēḇ biSĕdōm) is a pointed narrative note: Lot's location determined his fate — proximity to judgment brings judgment's collateral consequences.
13: "A man who had escaped (happāliṭ) came and reported this to Abram the Hebrew (haʿiḇrî)" — the first use of the word "Hebrew" (ʿiḇrî) in the Bible, designating Abram's ethnic-covenant identity. The term ʿiḇrî derives from ʿēḇer (the ancestor of the Hebrews, Genesis 10:21) and will become the standard designation for Israel in Egypt (Genesis 39:14; Genesis 40:15; Genesis 41:12; Exodus 1:15-19; Genesis 2:6-11). Abram's Amorite allies (Mamre, Eshkol, Aner) are introduced as "brothers" (baʿălê bĕrît ʾaḇrām, covenant partners of Abram) — literally "lords of Abram's covenant" — indicating a formal alliance relationship that crosses ethnic lines and demonstrates the covenant-community's extension beyond the blood-family.
14: "318 trained men born in his household (yĕlîḏê bêtô)" — the household-born warriors are servants trained from birth in Abram's household, distinct from hired soldiers or mercenaries. The number 318 is specific enough to suggest authentic historical tradition rather than a round number. A patriarch who could field 318 trained fighters commanded a substantial household-community — equivalent to a small chieftain's military force. Dan (the northern limit of the pursuit) is called by the name of the later Israelite tribe (Joshua 19:47; Judges 18:29 — the Danites renamed the city Laish as Dan) — a geographic landmark that would have been recognizable to the text's later audience.
15: Abram's night raid — dividing his forces and attacking at night — is a sound ANE tactical decision. Night attacks maximized surprise and compensated for numerical disadvantage (318 trained fighters against a returning army of four kingdoms). The same night-attack tactic is used by Gideon (Judges 7:9-22) and Jonathan (1 Samuel 14:31). The pursuit as far as Hobah "north of Damascus" — Damascus being the major city of southern Syria — indicates the eastern coalition had retreated far northward before Abram's forces caught them. The rescue operation covers the full geographic scope of the original campaign reversed.
16: "He recovered all the goods and brought back his relative Lot and his possessions, together with the women and the other people" — Abram's rescue is comprehensive: goods, Lot, women, other people. The scope of the rescue goes beyond the kinship obligation that motivated it — Abram recovers all the captives of Sodom, not just his nephew. This complete recovery positions Abram as a liberator for the entire region, not merely a family-member pursuing a kinsman-rescue (gōʾēl) obligation. The recovered women and other people of Sodom are the group whose fate will create the diplomatic tension with the king of Sodom in vv21-24.
17: The Valley of Shaveh — "the King's Valley" (ʿēmeq haššāwēh hûʾ ʿēmeq hammelek) — is identified in 2 Samuel 18:18 with a valley near Jerusalem where Absalom erected a memorial pillar. The Kidron valley (east of Jerusalem) is the traditional identification. The king of Sodom's coming out to meet Abram is a formal royal welcome for a returning military victor — the protocol for greeting a successful warrior. The meeting sets up the diplomatic encounter of vv21-24, where the king of Sodom will offer Abram the standard victor's reward and Abram will refuse it on covenant grounds.
18: Melchizedek ("my king is righteousness" or "righteous king" — malkî-ṣedeq) appears without genealogy, without predecessor, without successor — a priest-king outside any established priestly line. Salem (šālēm, peace/wholeness) is identified with Jerusalem in Psalms 76:2 ("His tent is in Salem, his dwelling place in Zion") — a connection that makes Melchizedek the first Jerusalemite priest-king in the biblical narrative. "Bread and wine" are covenant-hospitality elements — the meal as covenant ratification. ʾEl ʿElyôn (God Most High) is Abram's YHWH identified by Melchizedek's title: Abram's equation in v22 (where he swears by "YHWH, God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth") confirms these as the same deity. Melchizedek worships Abram's God.
19: Melchizedek's blessing of Abram — "blessed be Abram by God Most High, Creator (qōnēh) of heaven and earth" — uses the divine title qōnēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (Creator/Possessor of heaven and earth), a comprehensive sovereignty formula for the supreme deity. The Hebrews 7:7 comment — "the lesser is blessed by the greater" — applies here: Melchizedek blesses Abram as the superior party, establishing the Melchizedek-priesthood's precedence over the Abrahamic line. The blessing flows downward from priest-king to patriarch — a hierarchy that the later Levitical priesthood (descended from Abraham) cannot supersede.
20: "And praise be to God Most High, who delivered your enemies into your hand" — Melchizedek attributes the military victory to divine action; Abram is instrument, not cause. The tithe (maʿăśēr, a tenth) that Abram gives Melchizedek is the first tithe in the Bible — given to a non-Israelite, non-Levitical priest, outside any Mosaic law. The tithe as a recognition of divine ownership (one-tenth returned to the deity's representative acknowledges the remaining nine-tenths as held in stewardship) is a universal ANE practice. Abram's tithe to Melchizedek precedes the Levitical tithe law (Numbers 18:21-24) by centuries, establishing the tithe principle in the Abrahamic covenant itself — which is why Hebrews 7:9-10 can argue that Levi paid tithes to Melchizedek "in the loins of Abraham."
21: The king of Sodom's offer — "give me the people and keep the goods for yourself" — reflects the standard ANE victor's-reward arrangement. After a liberation/rescue, the people (as subjects of the original king) belonged to their king; the goods were the victor's spoils. The king of Sodom is offering the maximum possible reward under ANE convention. In contrast to Melchizedek's theological framing of the victory (divine deliverance), the king of Sodom offers a purely transactional resolution — Abram's rescue reduced to a military-commercial exchange. Abram's refusal of this transactional frame will be the passage's theological climax.
22: "With raised hand (hărîmōtî yādî) I have sworn an oath to the LORD, God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth" — the raised-hand gesture is the standard ANE oath posture, attested in Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Hittite contexts (cf. Exodus 6:8: YHWH "raised his hand" to swear the land oath to Israel; Deuteronomy 32:40). By explicitly equating YHWH with ʾEl ʿElyôn (Melchizedek's name for God), Abram confirms that the two men were worshipping the same deity under different names — the encounter was a genuine cross-cultural meeting of covenant-community members. The oath formula transforms a commercial negotiation into a covenant-loyalty statement.
23: "Not even a thread or the strap of a sandal" — the hyperbolic minimal-value formula (a thread and a sandal-strap are among the cheapest items in the ANE material culture) makes Abram's refusal absolute and emphatic. The refusal's stated rationale — "so that you will never be able to say, 'I made Abram rich'" — is the theological core: Abram's wealth comes from YHWH (already established in Genesis 12:2; Genesis 13:2), and accepting Sodom's king's goods would compromise that testimony. The covenant-identity (YHWH as patron/benefactor) must not be obscured by an alternative patronage narrative (king of Sodom as Abram's benefactor).
24: "I will accept nothing but what my men have eaten and the share that belongs to the men who went with me" — the exception for Aner, Eshkol, and Mamre (Abram's Amorite allies who fought with him) is a covenant-justice provision: they took the risk, they deserve the reward. Abram's personal covenant-commitment (no goods from Sodom) does not extend to his allies who have no such covenant obligation. The careful distinction between his personal oath-commitment and the legitimate claims of his coalition partners demonstrates the ANE principle that binding oaths create obligations only for the oath-taker, not for non-parties.
